regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: By Dr. J. J. Kane

regions.label: text
regions.text: Abphleite, La., July 21—Nelson Washington, who has been away from home for quite a while, spent a few days with friends and relatives. He returned to Oakdale, La., Tuesday. * Mrs. Mary Goodle, daughter of Lourdes, Honton, Houston, and Mrs. C. Chus, Goodle. * A pleasure-board ride was given by Messrs. C. Perry and C. Douglas to Shell Beach. He carried an enormous crowd. * Mrs. Frances saintard of Marlare, La., died on June 10, from a stroke. An hunt was given by the members of the St. Mary's Sunday school for the benefit of same, under the management of Miss Edna Camer, at the residence of Mrs. H. Dickens. * Mrs. Margaret saintard of Marlare, Miss Visitor丁. * M. Morrison and sister丁. were visitors of Mr. and Mrs. North Catleton. * Rev. W. S. Board, pastor of Pleasant Green Baptist Church, is conducting a revival. He has done executive duties in the church. * S. Jones, pastor of the M. E. Church of Herbert Cove, preached a grand session at Pleasant Green Baptist Church, which was appreciated by his hearers. * Mrs. Jones of New Orleans is in town to remember the death of the director of Tabernacles. * Mrs. Clara Blanched of New Iberia, La., district superintendent of Woman's Home Mission Society of the Congregational Church, is a very interesting lecture to an exegete. * Mrs. A. Herod, principal of Abbeville School, and the board of directors are petitioning to the people for their financial aid to liquidate the debt of the school. * A grand rally will be given by the officers of the church. * On July 30th, to help pay off the church debt, Rev. Frank Flowden, pastor.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: By T. G. Duncan

regions.label: text
regions.text:
West Monroe, La., July 21.-Mrs. E. L. Cox left for Waveland, Miss, last Saturday, where she will spend the summer on the const. * Mrs. G. S. Lee is still very H. * Rev. W. G. Head has been in Lille, La. Rev. W. G. Head, W. G. Kelby and M. F. Boby have returned from the Sunday school convention at Bernice, La. * The Young Men's Educational and Social Club gave a "tackey party" last Saturday night. * The Willing Workers Club met at the home of Mrs. L. Cox Wednesday, July 12.

St. Joe, La., July 21.-The members of the new salvation B. C. gave a picnic for the benefit of the new church, which was a success. There were four prizes, the one pulling the most cabbage, second the most gentleman, third to the best dressed lady and the fourth to the one catching the pigeon. Mr. Samuel Simpson won first and fourth prizes, C. J. McGivens second and Miss Laila Strand, Honey Island, a few years ago had a good time. A few weeks ago others gave the pastor, Rev. F. D. Robinson, a new suit.

Alton, La., July 21.-Mrs. Phyllis Lee, New Orleans, was the guest of Mrs. E. F. Johnson the past week. She is having a good time, she says.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: MAKES APPEAL FOR

regions.label: text
regions.text:
CASEMENTS RELEASE

Miami, Fla., July 21—Dr. A. P. Holly, formerly of Hayt, is the father of a movement to save the life of Sir Roger Casement (white), who is sentenced to death for high treason in England for bringing in exposes of the Belgian atrocities in the 'Congo rubber country, where some of the most awful crimes of the past years have been committed. While in the consular service he felt as if he had been a victim of the United Kingdom, England or Scotland and Ireland, and had prosecuted the stand he thought was right. For this action he has been sentenced to life imprisonment. The fact that Sir Roger Casement fought so bravely against atrocities in the Congo, Dr. Holly is urging members of the Race to take the initiative in requesting the British ambassador to forward an urgent appeal to the authorities in favor of the benefactor of the Race. Dr. Holly now devote his attention to the Race and appeal to the better class of whites to stop the lynching and " Jim树" raws in the south.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: ENTERTAIN YOUNG MEN

regions.label: text
regions.text: Columbia, Mo. July 21. —The Ladies' Art Club met with Mrs. Virgie Walden Wednesday afternoon, July 5. * Prof J. W. (Hind) Boone returned from Sedalia Friday in his Cole Six. * George Butler returned home from St. Paul, Minn., after an absence of fourteen years. Every one enjoys having him in our midst. * The Ames Johnson Chapter gave a musical concert at the second Friday, July 7. * It was an artistic treat, grand musical comedy was given by the Fulton Baptist choir at the Second Baptist Church of this city. "A Tale of the Hat." It was a grand affair and a large crowd was in attendance. * The greatest affair of the season was given by the young men of this city. "A Garden Party" in honor of Miss Dorothy Watkins in July, Mo. * The Peach costumed Miss Watkins and Misses Lillian and Edna Schurch with an auto ride Saturday. They report an enjoyable time. * Mrs. Josephine Brisco and daughter Louise, who have been visiting in Kentucky, returned home Saturday. * The young ladies of this city entertained the young men with a dance, Monday evening, July 10, at the Mackenzie Hall which was beautifully decorated with electrical Japanese lanterns. Music by Prof. T. M. Drew.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: MRS. DAWSON DEAD

regions.label: text
regions.text: Duluth, Minn, July 21—Mrs. William M. Dawson, aged 40, died at 10 o'clock Wednesday morning at St. Mary's hospital following an illness of about a year from cancer. She is survived by her husband, Mr. William M. Dawson, Minn. She is survived by her husband, Mr. William M. Dawson, Ohio. The funeral was held at 2 o'clock Friday afternoon from the residence, 620 Fifth avenue East. Rev George L. Holt, pastor of St. Mark's A. M. E. church, offered at the funeral services. The burial was made in Forest Hill cemetery, Mr. Dawson is very well known in the Spalding for many years, and now being head waiter at the New St. Louis hotel. * An interesting programme was given Tuesday night in the partors of St. Mark's A. M. E. church, Fifth avenue and Sixth street. Special effort was made by the pastor of the church to make the affair enjoyable. The programme included the following: Invocation by Rev James Higgins of Chicago, Ill.; welcome address by Rev George L. Holt; paper by Mrs. M. L. Jones of Wilmington, Del.; vocal solo by Mrs. George Moses of Lutson, Minn. * Inmate Mobil 10 East, Sixth street, returned the last of the week from the Twin Cities, where they attended the annual picnic of the St. Phillip's church of St. Paul, Minn.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: DAY NURSERY FUND STARTED

regions.label: text
regions.text: Philadelphia, Pa., July 21. —The Child Welfare League of Philadelphia announced after their meeting today that plans for the Day Nursery Fund were well under way. A generous start was secured through the concert here last week. The Philomathian Dramatic and Chorus Society of Philadelphia addr further to this sum through the prosecution of the Rose of Eden" at Fitzgerald's Auditorium at Atlantic City last Friday night. The efforts of the League are to be commended and the Defender wishes them success in this venture. Officers of the league are: Mrs. C. Jones, president; Mrs. N. L. Kemp, vice president; Mrs. Florence White, secretary; Mrs. Matthie Warrick, treasurer.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: HARRIS PASSES AWAY

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Norman W. Harris (white), wealthy the philanthropist and patron of education, died Sunday at his summer home, Wadsworth Hall, Lake Geneva, Wis. He was very much interested in Race Y. M. C. A. work. When the banquet was held at Old Yellow's Hall for the Wash-ash avenue camden Mr. Harris Cyrus McCormick and Julius Rosenwald, gave $25,000 apiece.

regions.label: text
regions.text: Ripley, Tennessee, July 21—The C. M. E. Church, of which Rev. R. W. Spearman, A. B. is pastor, was visited by the presiding elder of the Dyersburg district, West Tennessee conference. Dr. Rlivers touched two strong sermons, and the service day was successful. Prof. J. H. Kelly, the father of W. K. Kelly, pastor of the Holly Grove Baptist Church, is here visiting his son, Mrs. Cliffne Corner and Mrs. Sarah Mullins, formerly of this town, are here visiting the church and grandmother, Mrs. Florenta Curpin, Ferguson. * Mrs. Lelia Taylor, formerly of this town, but now resides in Memphis, is here visiting her parents, Mr. and Mrs. James Crook. Quite a jolly crowd, the children by the "Willing Workers" of the Holly Grove Baptist Church. The affair was largely attended by all and all reported a delightful time. * The children's day at last Sunday were Methodist Church on last Sunday were Methodist Church in due those who had the program in charge. * Rev. and Mrs. C. W. Kelly entertained at dinner Monday afternoon in honor of their father, Prof. J. H. Kelly in honor of their predecessor, Rev. Spearman, pastor of Were Church; Rev. M. Rivers, presiding elder of this district, and Prof. J. H. Kelly.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: By Mrs. Cassie L. Fowlkes

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Union G. T. Carlisle L. Powkes
Union G. T. Carlisle L. Powkes
Sarah Herring died July 13. She leaves to mourn her loss a husband, Mr. Oddie Herring; a father, Mr. John Wade; brothers, George Wade, Johnnie Wade Jr., Jenny Wade; Mrs. Mollie Capers, Hamilton, Mrs. Hugh Cravens, F. C. Crawden, shown by the ladies of the W. Y. E. Chalker, she was a member for eight years. The design prepared by this club was simply beautiful. Mrs. Herring was a member of the First Christian church and loved the First Christian church who knew her. Mrs. Nannie Clay, Herring who knew her, been the house guest of Mrs. B. L. Clarke the week end, will leave for her home Tuesday morning. *Miss Clay, a very sweet and quite an accomplished musician, has been much entertained by the society while here. *Mrs. W. P. Weston, who daughters passed through Saturday afternoon route to Chicago, Ill.; Washington, D.C. and other points, where they will spend the summer. *Mrs. Cora Cooper was the lovely hostess of the Bonton Literary Society day afternoon. Club was opened by the ladies of Mrs. A. E. Patton. Election was as follows: Mrs. A. B. Patton, president; Mrs. Cora Cooper, vice president; Mrs. Nannie Brown, secretary; Mrs. Viola Taylor, assistant secretary; Mrs. Fearl Long, treasurer; Mrs. E. L. Reporter. Club adjourned to meet with T. Foelman. *Mrs. Morgan Wells has turned from a very pleasant visit to mother, relatives and friends in Brownsville, Teen. Mrs. Nannie Adkins has come from Nashville, Teen. Mrs. Nannie Herring, husband of the late Mrs. Sarah Herring, too late to see his wife alive. She preparing to join him in Terre Haute, in a few days, their future home.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: By Crawford L. Robinson

regions.label: text
regions.text: Dyersburg, Tenn. July 21. - Miss Johnie Locks her home after a few weeks visit in our city. * Among those who have returned home from summer schools at the A. & L. State Normal were Mamesles L. J. Roundtree, A. Cross and L. Tom Fowkes and she is tenile L. Buckett and Alma E. Millard and P. Moser M. L. Morrison. * Miss Odessa M. L. will spend last week for Memphis, where she will spend her summer vacation. * The Doll W. B. seems to be well rushed by the Dude F. G. since the departure of the Doll Miss Tyus. * T. G. Robinson, superintendent, the National Life Insurance company, was in Humboldt on business last week. * Hoyer Roose. * City is the guest of Oner L. Thomas Jr. on East Court. * Blackberry pickling seems to have become the real sport of the day around here. Race people should delight in supporting Race enterners, the National subscribe for the Defender. C. L. Roberson, the East Court street. Phone 428-1 or 656. * No papers can be sold on credit. * Several dudes say they certainly enjoyed reading the letters from the Doll W. B. while she was on her vacation. * Mrs. Percy Sanderson has returned home from Upson to see few days' visit. * Mrs. A. D. Woods has club Friday last. * Mrs. Alma L. Burrow of Union City is in our city again. * Mrs. Tom Nealy has returned home after a few weeks' visit with friends and relatives in Mercer.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: By Fred H. Loster

regions.label: text
regions.text: Memphis, Tenn. July 21—On last Tuesday night, July 15, John's Baptist church received their first official notice that the preachers—there being two of them—the deacon and a part of the trustee board, the pastor and the treasurer will and accord, purchased the old Reeves church at a cost of $14,100, paid $4,200 and made out notes bearing 8 per cent the remainder. This church has been condemned by the city twice and it will cost not less than $3,000 to put it in a good condition. Furthermore, they have given the present structure, which is uniquely new. This deal was made at Mason Tennessee, the first part of this year, and has been denied every time when asked about it. The pastor emphatically denied the assertion to the writer of this article, who is a member of that church, and his 50 days ago. After the pastor returned his days ago, the same as if the church had sent the deacon and the trustees, and they split because they did not want the church to know anything about it. The underminders went on, ignored the members, appointed their committees, held their secret meetings in the church, and the convention at Clarksville he called the deacon and last Tuesday night brought in a report to be adopted, which read as stated above, and permit both to speak on the negative side. Those in the affirmative report at will. Many got disengaged and left, and when they finally agreed to the question the majority adopted the report. Now the boys wishes to say that St. John's year running smoothly for 35 years. Twenty-seven years under the pastorship of Rev. Wm. J. McMichiel, many have put in their penny at various times, and in the church the church and the community have been convinced that the community are men with about one share in the local bank that is handling the affair. They are trying to make good with the officials. The pastors went to get their names on the paper to cinch their hands and impose a debt on the church to finish paying. Show me where they is any Christianity in that. And they intend to carry their plans out if it splits the church. * On Wednesday last the three commanderies of this city had a joint installation. Sir W. R. Snipes, grand recorder, acted as grand eminent, and also signed a nified manner that it has been consented that it was the grandest affair of its kind ever had in the city. He was assisted by the grand captain general, Sir H. M. Brockington, who is also of exceptional ability. The three auxiliaries were out in large numbers and were deceived by the church, as well as nified manner that it has been consented that it was the grandest affair of its kind good things. * Mr. and Mrs. Marion Dixon delightfully entertained 50 children in honor of their little 2-year-old girl, in East End last Tuesday morning, and Mrs. J. E. Mason, Little Rock, Ark. in the city visiting Mr. Mason's brother, Ernest Mason, 785 Walker avenue, but expect to leave in a few days to points of interest in the north. * Mr. Mason

regions.label: text
regions.text: Humboldt, Tenn., July 21.—Two of the most enjoyable affairs of the season were those given in honor of Mrs. C. C. Adams. Mife of the ex-pastor of St. Mary's night night Mrs. Ella Bludes was hostess at a delightful affair when she entertained au茶 in honor of Mrs. Adams. * Mr. Alphonse Moore, Henry Williams and Jardill Jones made a trip to Jacksonville and Jacksonville, Florida. They are the brick masonns on the new custom house that is being erected in this city. * Mrs. Edna Wheeler and Mrs. Queenena Harman were visiting in Jacksonville Sunday. Mrs. Edna Wheeler and Mrs. Queenena Harman are Jackson, the guest of Jim Studovant. * Miss Edna Mai Sharp left for St. Louis Sunday night to visit her mother. * Macauley Bolton, Memphis, is in the city visiting home folks. * Mrs. Annie Ivory, Fulton, KY, is visiting home folks.

regions.label: text
regions.text: McKenzie, Tenn., July 21.—The Rev. Harris of the C. M. E. church delivered a very fine sermon on last Sunday morning. Rev. Mr. Craig prescribed an excellent sermon in the evening. *Mr. and Mrs. W. M. Bell were the guests of Dr. W. M. Bell, Martin J. M., Sunday. *Mrs. Robert Johnson, M., attended the state examination at Huntingdon last week. Miss Frerson was in Huntingdon Friday and Saturday in the interest of the industrial work which she represents. *Prof. N. W. Love made a trip trip to Huntingdon on business visiting his sister James. who has been visiting his sister since last week for Cairo, St. Louis and other points of interest. Mrs. Amanda Scott and daughter will return to Mounds Thursday, accompanied by Mrs. Viola, Edwards and little Hazel Thomas. Mrs. Anne Gregory will have a new home on Booker avenue July 1st. Mr. and Mrs. Homer Dixon attended services at Cherry Wood Sunday. They were the dinner guests of Mr. and Mrs. Robert Johnson. *Rev. T. F. Wilson preached an excellent sermon at Cherry Wood Sunday. The attendance was fine. Mrs. Robert Johnson, Cherry Wood, are on the last week. *Mrs. Queen Dunwiddle continues very ill. *Mrs. Add Sneed arrived from Cincinnati Thursday, where she has been visiting her sister. *Mrs. Charlie Britt and infant daughter have returned to Cincinnati and will have her husband, Mr. Britt. Hickman Ky. Guy Barker, who have been visiting their parents, Mr. and Mrs. Alex Barker, this place, have returned to their home in Mounts. III. *Mesdames Chas.

regions.text: [OCR timeout]

regions.label: text
regions.text: Henderson, KY. July 21.—The quarterly meeting was held at the Free Will Baptist church and there was a good attendance. * Mrs. Marie Rankins, 83 Logan street, is very ill. Under the care of Dr. S. A. O'Neal. * Mrs. Marie Gaines, Seventh street, is quite ill, under the care of Dr. Wilson. * Mrs. Sadie E. Blevins, having a few weeks in Louisville, Ky. come home Sunday for a short stay with parents, Mr. and Mrs. Adolphus Meckel, Seventh street. She was greeted by all who knew her. Miss Blevins has returned to Louisville. * Porter Hayes and Mr. John M. Taylor, St. Louis, are in visiting relatives and friends, Mr. Taylor, Mrs. Hayes, for the past ten years, and visits this city seldom. * Mr. and Mrs. John Gones, St. Louis, arrived in this city Sunday morning, giving quite a surprise to their many friends. About four years have clapped since Mrs. Gones was here on our visit. They were greeted by our friends. They were a most pleasant one. They returned St. Louis Sunday night. * Mrs. B. E Doxey and daughter were called to Chicago to visit the bedside of Mrs. Doxey's mother, Mrs. Anna Alexander, 532 Kenwood avenue. * Rev. J. R. Robinson attended the Baptist Woman's Missionary Society, St. KY. Rev. C. H. Arnett preached two days at Norris Chapel Baptist church. A grand entertainment was given Saturday night at the residence of Mrs. Molle Marshall. * Mr. Dan Valentine is on the sick list. * The age rattle at Morris Chapel was quite a success. * Mrs. Cora Thomas Brooks and her bed. * Mr. Thomas Brooks and her daughter, Ky., are in the city, the groom of brother, Mr. Sheby Brooks, on Third street. * Mrs. Martha Randolph, who has been ill, is much improved.
